_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the technical fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.cape: The Shiller CAPE (Cyclically Adjusted Price-to-Earnings) ratio divides current price by the 10-year inflation-adjusted average of earnings, smoothing earnings volatility to assess valuation. At 41.52 in July 2026, it stands well above its 140-year historical average of 17.3, exceeded only by 44.19 at the December 1999 dot-com peak. Elevated CAPE levels signal expensive equity valuations linked historically to lower subsequent long-term returns—above ~39, the average forward S&P 500 returns have been around -4% over one year and -20% over two years. However, CAPE timing is imperfect, as market returns can remain elevated or volatile, showing that valuation alone does not determine when the 'music stops.'
notes.buffett: The Buffett Indicator measures total US equity market capitalization as a percentage of GDP, representing equity market value relative to economic output. The current reading of 231.7% (June 2026) far surpasses previous peaks of 146% during the dot-com bubble and 110% before the 2007 financial crisis. Warren Buffett has described levels above 200% as 'playing with fire,' implying elevated systemic risk and potential for drawdowns given overextended valuations. Yet, like CAPE, this metric's limitation lies in timing: high ratios warn of market froth and risk but cannot predict precisely when liquidity conditions will trigger a sharp adjustment.
notes.concentration: Market concentration quantifies the weight of the largest stocks within an index, reflecting risk and potential fragility from reliance on few entities. In 2026, the top 5 S&P 500 names constitute approximately 29.8%, the top 10 form 41.0% compared to 17.5% in 2015, and the technology sector accounts for 48% of index weighting. Such concentration can amplify market vulnerability if key stocks or sectors experience sharp reversals, reducing diversification benefits and potentially precipitating sharper index declines when liquidity tightens. A caveat is that high concentration can persist alongside strong momentum, delaying corrections until liquidity constraints or sentiment shifts force reevaluation.
notes.yieldcurve: The yield curve spread between the 10-year Treasury and 3-month Treasury (10Y-3M) indicates monetary policy stance and economic expectations; a positive spread typically reflects growth optimism. At 0.7 points in July 2026, the spread is modestly positive, coinciding with a 31% NY Fed recession probability estimate. A flattening or inverted yield curve historically signals economic slowdown risk and can presage tightening liquidity conditions that end bull markets. Limitations include the yield curve's imperfect signal to timing market declines, as it reflects economic expectations but does not capture financial market liquidity shocks directly.
notes.drypowder: Dry powder refers to cash reserves held by investors or institutions, indicative of potential buying power or forced selling risk. Berkshire Hathaway's record cash position of $397.4 billion in Q1 2026 represents substantial liquidity ready to deploy, yet 79% of institutions anticipate a decline in cash holdings. High dry powder can cushion downturns or enable selective buying when the music stops, but a coordinated reduction in cash buffers implies increasing vulnerability to forced asset sales and reduced liquidity. The dynamic nature of dry powder makes it an uncertain timing signal: it indicates capacity but not when that liquidity will be prudently or urgently deployed.
notes.finale: Valuation metrics such as CAPE and the Buffett Indicator set the potential magnitude of a market correction, reflecting extreme overvaluation conditions known historically to precede negative returns. However, these measures lack precision in timing the market 'pause' or correction—analogous to the music stopping in musical chairs—caused by liquidity shocks rather than valuation alone. The forthcoming trigger window between December 2026 and May 2027, characterized by large IPO lock-up expirations from companies like SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic, represents a potential liquidity crisis as insiders may sell shares, increasing supply and pressuring prices. This liquidity event fits the frame where abundant momentum and cheap money sustain high valuations until a sudden liquidity withdrawal forces investors to exit positions, marking the market's inflection point.
